title: Catch Line Repair
desc: The handlines on a lower cliff path took a beating in last night's spray surge.

steps.text:
Not every route at Cordac Falls deserves the name road. Some are shelves carved into wet cliff, with room for one careful set of boots and a handline if the quartermaster has had a good month. The lower path to the hauling ledge took a hard spray surge overnight, and now one rope section is frayed while the catch net under the bend is hanging loose enough to make everyone suddenly respectful of maintenance work.

The line rigger drops a coil at your feet. "Splice the handline if you like rope work," she says. "Patch the net if you trust knots more than luck. Either way, I want one less reason for carriers to pray on their way down."

options.label: Splice the damaged handline and retension the run
Patch the catch net under the bend

steps.text:
The handline's damage is worst near the midpoint where spray keeps the braid wet and the rock edge keeps worrying at it. You cut out the weakened section, marry fresh rope into the existing run, and work the splice tight under the rigger's eye until the joint is clean enough to pass a gloved hand without snagging. Then comes the harder part: hauling the whole line back to proper tension without pulling the wall anchors out of their seats.

When the line finally sets, it runs straight and firm from post to post, no sag, no twist, no rotten give in the middle. The rigger tests it by leaning her full weight into the rope over the worst drop on the path. It holds. She nods once and says, "Good. If anyone slips now, they at least have the decency to stop where we intended."

options.label: Coil the cut ends and clear the path for carriers

steps.text:
The catch net hangs beneath the blind bend where the path narrows and the mist thickens, less because anyone plans to fall there than because Cordac Falls has strong opinions about plans. Two edge ties have snapped and one section of webbing has torn where a crate corner must have hit it on the way down. You crawl onto the maintenance bracket, thread fresh cord through the frame, and draw the net back into a proper pocket instead of the sad sag it had become.

Once the last knot is dressed, the rigger tosses a sandbag into the repaired section. The net dips, stretches, and catches the weight cleanly. Nothing glamorous about it. Just a practical piece of trust hanging over a long drop. The rigger retrieves the bag, satisfied. "That will do. No one notices a good net until they need it. Then they remember it forever."

options.label: Climb back to the path and leave the safety net ready
